documentation.value: Logs an error message to the Apple System Log facility. Uses printf-style format specifiers with %@ for objects.
An NSString format string (e.g., @"Value: %@, Count: %d").
Variable arguments matching the format specifiers.
Returns a string created by using a given format string as a template into which the remaining argument values are substituted.
A format string. Uses %@ for objects, %d for integers, %f for floats, etc.
A comma-separated list of arguments to substitute into the format string.
Creates and returns an array containing the objects in the argument list. The list must be nil-terminated.
The first object to include in the array.
Additional objects, followed by nil to terminate the list.
Creates a dictionary with object-key pairs from the argument list. Must be nil-terminated.
The value for the first key.
The key for the first object.
Additional value-key pairs, terminated with nil.
Allocates memory for a new object and initializes it. The standard two-step Objective-C object creation pattern.
The class to instantiate.
Initializes and returns a newly allocated UIView object with the specified frame rectangle.
The frame rectangle (CGRect) for the view, specifying origin and size in the superview's coordinate system.
Adds a view to the end of the receiver's list of subviews.
The view to add as a subview. It is retained by the receiver.
Invokes a method of the receiver on the current thread after a specified delay.
A selector identifying the method to invoke. The method should take one or no arguments.
The argument to pass to the method. Pass nil if the method takes no arguments.
The minimum time before the message is sent, in seconds.
Submits a block for asynchronous execution on a dispatch queue and returns immediately.
The dispatch queue to submit the block to (e.g., dispatch_get_main_queue(), dispatch_get_global_queue()).
The block to execute asynchronously. It takes no arguments and has no return value.
